description: Quality and correctness rules for offline three.js WebGL2 3D games built from the web-components-3d Lego catalog. Covers the local-importmap-only loading contract (no CDN, no network, runs under file://), draw-call and DPR budgets, delta-time movement, dispose-on-teardown and object pooling, mobile light limits, hand-rolled physics versus rigid bodies, audio unlock on first user gesture, and WebGL2-not-WebGPU. These rules are for 3D games only and must not leak into productivity mini-apps.

guidelines.rule: The importmap MUST be LOCAL and relative only. Map the bare specifier 'three' to './resources/three/three.module.min.js' and the PREFIX 'three/addons/' to './resources/three/addons/'. NEVER reference any https:// URL, CDN, unpkg, jsdelivr, esm.sh, skypack, or es-module-shims. Any 'https://' inside the importmap or in an import statement is an instant failure.
guidelines.why: These games ship as static Hub bundles and run under file:// inside WKWebView, which is not a secure context and has no network guarantee. A CDN importmap renders a blank canvas offline. The local prefix mapping for 'three/addons/' is mandatory so addon-to-addon cross-imports (EffectComposer pulling Pass, RenderPass, shaders) resolve locally instead of hitting the network.
examples: <script type="importmap">{ "imports": { "three": "./resources/three/three.module.min.js", "three/addons/": "./resources/three/addons/" } }</script>
Game code uses canonical specifiers: import * as THREE from 'three'; import { OrbitControls } from 'three/addons/controls/OrbitControls.js';
WRONG: "three": "https://unpkg.com/three@0.160.0/build/three.module.js" - fails the local-importmap gate
WRONG: <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/es-module-shims"> - native importmaps are guaranteed on the target webviews, do not add a shim

guidelines.rule: The importmap block MUST appear in <head> BEFORE the first module <script>. Place it ahead of any import so the resolver sees the mappings when the first module loads.
guidelines.why: An importmap declared after a module script has already started resolving is ignored, and 'Failed to resolve module specifier three' kills the whole scene. Order is load-bearing, not cosmetic.
examples: Head order: <meta charset> then <style> then <script type="importmap"> then <script type="module" src="./game.js">
Never inject the importmap from JS after DOMContentLoaded - it is too late

guidelines.rule: Use the WebGL2 path only. Construct three.js WebGLRenderer (never WebGPURenderer). If TSL or node materials are used they MUST compile to the GLSL/WebGL backend. GPGPU float-texture sims must capability-check EXT_color_buffer_float and degrade gracefully when absent.
guidelines.why: file:// is not a secure context, so even Safari 26 silently drops WebGPU - WebGPURenderer produces a permanently blank canvas in the shipped bundle. WebGLRenderer is the only renderer that survives the offline file:// target. Float-texture GPGPU effects (flow field, boids, reaction-diffusion) crash without the float color-buffer extension on weaker mobile GPUs.
examples: const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ antialias: true, powerPreference: 'high-performance' });
WRONG: new THREE.WebGPURenderer(...) - blank under file://
if (!renderer.capabilities.isWebGL2) { /* show a friendly message, do not crash */ }
const floatOK = renderer.getContext().getExtension('EXT_color_buffer_float'); if (!floatOK) useCPUFallback();

guidelines.rule: Keep the scene under 100 draw calls. Merge static geometry, use InstancedMesh for repeated objects (obstacles, asteroids, projectiles, creeps, particles), and share materials. Watch renderer.info.render.calls and treat it as a budget, not a number to discover after shipping.
guidelines.why: Each draw call is CPU-bound submission overhead; on mobile GPUs hundreds of individual meshes drop the game below 30fps even when the polygon count is tiny. Instancing draws thousands of identical objects in one call. The web-components-3d systems Lego (instanced-obstacle-field, instanced-asteroid-field, instanced-projectiles) exist specifically so genre code never spawns one Mesh per entity.
examples: const field = new THREE.InstancedMesh(geo, mat, 500); // one draw call for 500 obstacles
Reuse a single shared material across all instances; do not clone a material per object
Log renderer.info.render.calls in dev and assert it stays under 100
Merge static level geometry with BufferGeometryUtils.mergeGeometries at load time

guidelines.rule: Clamp devicePixelRatio. Call renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2)) and re-apply on resize. Never render at the raw DPR of a 3x Retina phone.
guidelines.why: A 3x DPR phone renders 9x the pixels of a 1x display; on a fragment-heavy 3D scene that is the difference between 60fps and a slideshow. Clamping to 2 keeps the image crisp while capping the pixel budget. This is the single highest-ROI mobile performance fix.
examples: renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2));
On resize: renderer.setSize(w, h); renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2));
WRONG: renderer.setPixelRatio(window.devicePixelRatio) with no clamp

guidelines.rule: All movement, physics, timers, and animation MUST be delta-time based. Use a fixed-timestep accumulator: accumulate real elapsed seconds, step update(dt) at a fixed dt (e.g. 1/60), clamp dt to avoid a spiral of death after a tab stall, and interpolate the render. Never advance state by a constant per-frame amount.
guidelines.why: Frame rate varies across devices (30, 60, 120Hz) and stalls when a tab backgrounds. Per-frame movement makes a 120Hz phone run the game twice as fast as a 60Hz one and teleports everything after a stall. Fixed-timestep with a clamped accumulator gives identical, deterministic motion on every device.
examples: position.x += speed * dt; // not position.x += speed
acc += Math.min(rawElapsed, 0.1); while (acc >= STEP) { update(STEP); acc -= STEP; }
Clamp the raw frame delta to ~0.1s so a backgrounded tab does not produce one giant catch-up step
core.three-game-loop already implements this accumulator - import it instead of hand-rolling rAF

guidelines.rule: The render loop MUST increment window.__threeFrameCount once per rendered frame. Initialize it to 0 and bump it inside the animation callback right after renderer.render(...).
guidelines.why: The macOS InstalledMiniAppRenderProbe reads window.__threeFrameCount to confirm the WebGL scene is actually rendering frames (frameCount greater than 0), not just sitting on a static first frame or a silently failed context. Omitting this counter makes a working game fail the render probe and get flagged unplayable.
examples: window.__threeFrameCount = 0; // at startup
function animate() { update(dt); renderer.render(scene, camera); window.__threeFrameCount++; }
renderer.setAnimationLoop(animate); // the loop that bumps the counter

guidelines.rule: Dispose every GPU resource on teardown. Geometries, materials, textures, render targets, and the renderer itself must be explicitly disposed when the game ends or the view is torn down. Walk the scene and call .dispose() on geometry, material(s), and any map/texture, then renderer.dispose().
guidelines.why: three.js does not garbage-collect GPU memory. Geometries, textures, and render targets live in WebGL until you dispose them; a game that rebuilds its scene on restart without disposing leaks VRAM until the context is lost and the canvas goes black. Mini-apps get torn down and relaunched repeatedly in the host, so the leak compounds fast.
examples: scene.traverse(o => { o.geometry?.dispose(); const m = o.material; (Array.isArray(m) ? m : [m]).forEach(mat => { mat?.map?.dispose(); mat?.dispose(); }); });
renderTarget.dispose(); composer?.dispose?.(); renderer.dispose();
Remove resize / visibilitychange / pointer listeners in the same dispose() path
core.three-game-loop exposes a dispose() - call it from the host teardown hook

guidelines.rule: Pool particles, projectiles, and short-lived objects. Pre-allocate a fixed pool (or a single InstancedMesh) and recycle entries instead of creating and destroying meshes/geometries every frame or every shot. Route all particles through effects.instanced-particle-pool.
guidelines.why: Allocating a Mesh, geometry, or material per projectile or per particle every frame triggers constant GC pauses and the dispose churn of tjs-008. A fixed pool keeps allocation flat: spawning a bullet just activates a free slot and resets its transform; despawning returns it to the pool. This is what keeps bullet-hell and particle bursts at a steady frame rate.
examples: const pool = []; for (let i=0;i<256;i++) pool.push(makeBullet()); // pre-allocated, hidden until used
spawn(): take a dead slot, set position/velocity, mark alive; despawn(): mark dead, hide, return to pool
Particles: one additive Points or InstancedMesh, update positions in a typed array, never one mesh per spark
Never new THREE.Mesh(...) inside the per-frame update loop

guidelines.rule: Cap active lights at 3 on mobile. Prefer a single DirectionalLight (sun) plus one HemisphereLight (sky/ground fill); bake or fake the rest. Limit shadow-casting to one light and use a blob/baked shadow for everything else. Do not add a PointLight per object.
guidelines.why: Each real-time light multiplies fragment-shader cost, and each shadow-casting light adds a full extra render pass. Four or more dynamic lights with shadows tank mobile GPUs. The scene-rig recipe (1 DirectionalLight + 1 HemisphereLight) plus blob shadows looks great and stays cheap. Per-object PointLights are the classic way an otherwise simple scene drops to single-digit fps.
examples: scene.add(new THREE.DirectionalLight(0xffffff, 2.0)); scene.add(new THREE.HemisphereLight(0xbfd4ff, 0x40402a, 0.6));
Only the DirectionalLight casts shadows; everything else uses effects.blob-shadow
WRONG: attaching a PointLight to each collectible or enemy
core.three-scene-rig already wires the 2-light recipe - reuse it

guidelines.rule: Hand-roll movement and collision unless the genre genuinely needs rigid-body dynamics. Use simple kinematics (capsule sweep, AABB/sphere overlap, raycast ground snap) for runners, racers, platformers, flight, tower defense, and rhythm games. Only pull systems.rapier-physics-world for genres whose fun IS emergent rigid-body physics (stacking, tumbling, ragdoll, knock-down).
guidelines.why: Rapier-compat adds ~1.9MB of embedded wasm to every bundle and a full physics step to every frame. For a lane runner or an on-rails racer that overhead buys nothing - a few lines of kinematic math are smaller, faster, and easier to tune. Reserve the one physics-engine Lego for games where you actually want bodies to collide, topple, and settle unpredictably.
examples: Runner/racer/flight/TD/rhythm: kinematic update + sphere or AABB overlap checks, no engine
Platformer ground snap: a single downward raycast, not a physics body
Stacking / tumbling / domino / ragdoll: import systems.rapier-physics-world (embedded-wasm, offline)
Do not add rapier to a bundle that never calls world.step()

guidelines.rule: Unlock audio on the first real user gesture. AudioContext (and three.js AudioListener / PositionalAudio) boots suspended; on the first tap/click/keydown call audioContext.resume() and play a 1-sample silent buffer, THEN start sound. Always ship a visible tap-to-start / Play control. If audio cannot start, keep the game fully playable and visually intact, just muted.
guidelines.why: iOS and WKWebView require a genuine user gesture to start audio; calling resume() at load does nothing and leaves the game permanently silent. The silent-buffer trick fully unlocks the context on iOS. Gating sound start behind a Play button is the reliable pattern. Audio must never block rendering - a muted game still renders, so degrade gracefully rather than stalling on a suspended context.
examples: playButton.addEventListener('pointerdown', () => { audioCtx.resume(); playSilentSample(audioCtx); startGame(); }, { once: true });
playSilentSample: const b = ctx.createBuffer(1,1,22050); const s = ctx.createBufferSource(); s.buffer=b; s.connect(ctx.destination); s.start(0);
three.js: const listener = new THREE.AudioListener(); camera.add(listener); resume the listener.context on first tap
If audioCtx.state stays 'suspended', continue rendering muted - never block the render loop on audio

guidelines.rule: If the goal is a GAME, ship a COMPLETE PLAYABLE core loop on first load, not just an ambient scene: the player takes an action, sees an immediate consequence, and can reach a win OR lose state by playing. A pretty scene with only idle motion and no interaction is a FAILURE for a game goal.
guidelines.why: 3D builds routinely render a beautiful scene but forget the game (no reachable objective, no working enemies, no win/lose). The taste rubric and render-gate both PASS a blank-but-pretty canvas, so nothing else catches it. Playability is the actual product.
examples: Shooter: enemies spawn + advance, firing spawns projectiles that hit + kill them, score climbs, health to 0 = Game Over
Runner: obstacles approach, a collision ends the run, distance/score climbs
WRONG: a starfield + a ship that only idles, no visible enemies, no way to win or lose

guidelines.rule: Every gameplay entity the player reacts to (enemies, targets, hazards, collectibles) MUST be VISIBLE in the camera view at start or spawn within ~2s, and MUST actually move/respond: enemies path toward or attack the player, projectiles travel and register hits, collectibles vanish + score on pickup. A static, off-screen, or inert entity is a failure.
guidelines.why: Players reported 'I can't see an enemy or evade' — entities were never spawned or placed outside the frame, so there was nothing to play against. If the player cannot SEE and INTERACT with the entities, the game is unplayable no matter how nice the scene.
examples: FPS/dogfight: enemies enter the camera frustum, visibly approach/shoot; crosshair + on-hit feedback confirm hits
Collectathon: gems sit in view on the reachable path, vanish + score on contact
WRONG: enemies spawned behind the camera or never spawned

guidelines.rule: Every control you document (WASD, arrows, mouse-look, click-to-fire, space-to-jump, drag) MUST be wired to a handler that visibly changes the scene the SAME frame it is pressed: add the listeners, update an input state, apply it in the update loop. Mentally test each key — does pressing it produce an immediate on-screen response?
guidelines.why: A frequent failure is keys/buttons with NO attached handler (the evaluator's 'NO HANDLER ATTACHED' finding): the HUD shows controls but nothing happens. Unresponsive controls make the game unplayable even when it renders.
examples: keydown/keyup set input flags; the fixed-step update reads them and moves the player; camera follows
click → pointer-lock; pointermove rotates the view (mouse-look)
WRONG: a 'Jump' button or 'W' key with no addEventListener / no state mutation

guidelines.rule: Wire score/health/collision/timer so the player can ACTUALLY reach the win state by playing well and the lose state by failing — both reachable — and show a clear overlay ('You Win!' / 'Game Over') with a working Restart. Avoid instant-fail, unreachable goals, or no end at all.
guidelines.why: A game with no win/lose, or an unreachable objective, has no stakes and no completion — it reads as a tech demo, not a game. The end states + restart are what make it feel finished and playable.
examples: health to 0 → Game Over overlay + Restart; reach the goal / survive the timer / clear all waves → You Win
WRONG: a score counter that never triggers any end state

guidelines.rule: Aim the camera so the player can SEE what they must react to: enemies approaching, the path/obstacles ahead, collectibles, the goal. First-person: keep threats entering the forward view. Third-person/chase: frame the player plus nearby threats with lookahead. Top-down/orbit: keep the whole play area legible.
guidelines.why: Even with working entities + controls, a camera pointed away from the action (or too close, or not following) hides the gameplay so the player can't react — indistinguishable from broken.
examples: chase camera trails the player with lookahead toward oncoming obstacles
FPS camera at eye height, enemies spawning ahead/around (not behind)
WRONG: a fixed camera on empty sky while the action happens off-screen

guidelines.rule: The instructions / how-to-play / controls panel MUST be collapsible: give it a minimize button that shrinks it to a small round info ('i') icon in the same corner (click the icon to expand), and optionally auto-collapse after ~9 seconds. Prefer the reusable ui.info-panel component (createInfoPanel({title, html, corner, autoCollapseMs})). Keep the score/HUD compact and separate. The instructions panel must NOT permanently cover a large part of the scene.
guidelines.why: Generated instruction boxes routinely cover a third of the screen and break immersion (real user feedback on Labyrinth-3D). A one-click minimize-to-icon keeps the how-to-play available without dominating the view.
